feed.title: Emily Maxwell Outland
feed.subtitle: Graduate researcher at CU Boulder working on explainable AI, human-machine teaming, and bioastronautics.

entry.title: What Jiu Jitsu Taught Me About Not Understanding Things

link.title: What Jiu Jitsu Taught Me About Not Understanding Things

entry.content:
This is a sample personal essay seeding the home page card. Replace it with your own writing whenever you’re ready — just edit this file or create a new one in _posts/.

I started Brazilian Jiu Jitsu because I was told it was a good idea to know how to defend yourself. I stayed because there is almost nothing in my life that provides the same immediate, undeniable feedback as being submitted by someone half my size.

In research, you can tell yourself a half-formed idea is almost there. You can sit with uncertainty for months. The model might be wrong but the data haven’t caught up yet. There’s always another interpretation.

On the mat, you either escaped or you didn’t.

I’m still mostly in the “didn’t” category. But I’m learning that being bad at something — truly, visibly, physically bad — is one of the more honest experiences available. You can’t theorize your way out of a triangle choke.

I think there’s something in that worth holding onto, especially in a field that rewards having a sophisticated take on everything.
entry.summary: There's something clarifying about being very bad at something physical. A sample personal essay — replace with your own writing.

entry.title: On Making Robots Explain Themselves
entry.content:
This is a sample research note seeding the home page card. Replace it with your own writing whenever you’re ready — just edit this file or delete it and create a new post in _posts/.

The question at the center of my work right now is deceptively simple: what does it mean for an autonomous system to explain itself?

When a path planner routes around an obstacle, the decision is legible to the engineer who built the system. It’s usually invisible to the person depending on it. A saliency heatmap makes the geometry visible, but “the robot went left because this region had high density” isn’t the same as understanding why that path was the right choice — or whether to trust it.

The gap between interpretable and understandable is where a lot of human-robot trust actually breaks down. I’m spending most of my time thinking about that gap.

More to come once I have something concrete to say.
entry.summary: What does it actually mean for a robot to explain itself? A placeholder research note — replace with your first real post.
